skills.title: Hip Bath (Cold / Neutral / Hot)
indication: Chronic constipation and functional bowel disorders
contraindication: Active menstrual bleeding (cold hip bath)
Uncontrolled hypertension (hot variant)
Open perineal wounds or recent perineal surgery
Severe peripheral vascular disease
technique: Step 1: Fill hip bath tub to navel level — cold (15–18°C), neutral (32–36°C), or hot (40–45°C) as prescribed. Ensure tub is sanitised with dilute Dettol between patients.
Step 2: Assist patient to sit in tub so only the pelvic region is submerged; feet and legs remain outside on a footrest or stool.
Step 3: Cover the patient's upper body and legs with a dry sheet or blanket to prevent heat loss and maintain modesty.
Step 4: Maintain water temperature throughout the procedure by adding warm/cold water as needed; monitor with bath thermometer every 5 minutes.
Step 5: Observe patient for dizziness, pallor, or discomfort; ask for verbal feedback at each temperature check.
Step 6: At end of prescribed time, assist patient out, pat the perineal area dry, and advise rest for 10–15 minutes.
skills.duration: 10–20 minutes (cold: 10 min; neutral: 20 min; hot: 10–15 min)
skills.frequency: Once or twice daily as prescribed; alternate cold/hot on successive days for contrast effect
skills.bnys_tip: For chronic constipation, cold hip bath (15–18°C) for 10 minutes in the morning on an empty stomach stimulates peristalsis more effectively than laxatives and avoids dependency. Ensure patient voids bladder before treatment.
skills.india_note: In summer months across South India, tap water may already be 28–30°C; always verify with thermometer before classifying as 'cold.' In North India winters, heating water to neutral temperature is critical to avoid vasovagal responses in elderly patients.

skills.title: Spinal Bath
indication: Spinal cord fatigue and vertebral column tensions
contraindication: Active spinal fracture or recent spinal surgery
Severe osteoporosis with vertebral fragility
Active febrile illness
Marked hypotension (cold variant)
technique: Step 1: Fill the spinal bath trough (approximately 180 cm × 60 cm × 15 cm deep) to a depth of 8–10 cm with water at prescribed temperature — cold (15–20°C), neutral (32–35°C), or hot (38–42°C).
Step 2: Instruct the patient to lie supine in the trough so only the back from occiput to coccyx is submerged; shoulders and buttocks rest on the trough edges.
Step 3: Place a waterproof neck pillow or folded towel under the occiput to prevent neck strain.
Step 4: Cover the anterior body (chest and abdomen) with a dry cotton sheet for warmth and modesty.
Step 5: Maintain water level and temperature throughout; replenish with a thin stream of water if temperature drifts more than 1°C.
Step 6: Assist the patient to sit up slowly post-treatment to prevent orthostatic hypotension; document blood pressure before and after in hypertensive patients.
skills.frequency: Daily for acute conditions; alternate days for chronic management
equipment: Spinal bath trough (dedicated fibre/porcelain trough or adapted immersion bath)
skills.bnys_tip: Cold spinal bath activates the sympathetic chains along the paravertebral ganglia, producing a measurable reduction in blood pressure within 20 minutes. Document pre- and post-procedure BP readings to track therapeutic response objectively.
skills.india_note: Many naturopathic hospitals in India use custom-built cement or tiled spinal troughs. Ensure the grouting is intact and non-porous to prevent bacterial colonisation. Chlorine tablet (0.5 ppm) added to water is acceptable for institutional use.

skills.title: Arm Bath
indication: Bronchial asthma (hot arm bath — derivative effect)
Angina pectoris (mild hot arm bath under supervision)
Peripheral vascular disorders of upper limb
Rheumatoid arthritis of wrist and elbow joints
contraindication: Acute eczema or open skin lesions on arms
Uncontrolled cardiac arrhythmia (hot variant)
Deep vein thrombosis of upper extremity
Severe sensory neuropathy (temperature perception impaired)
technique: Step 1: Prepare two tubs or basins — one hot (40–44°C) and one cold (15–18°C) for contrast, or a single basin at prescribed temperature.
Step 2: Seat the patient comfortably with the arm(s) resting on a padded armrest at tub level; position tub at elbow height to avoid shoulder strain.
Step 3: Submerge both arms to just below the axilla; confirm temperature tolerance verbally.
Step 4: For contrast arm bath: alternate 3–4 minutes hot, 1 minute cold, repeated 3–4 cycles; end on cold for tonic effect or on warm for relaxation.
Step 5: During hot arm bath for asthma: monitor respiratory rate and SpO₂ if pulse oximeter is available; observe for bronchospasm relief.
Step 6: Pat arms dry with a soft towel, especially in interdigital spaces; advise patient to remain seated for 5 minutes after treatment.
skills.frequency: Once daily; contrast arm bath alternate days
equipment: Two basin tubs or dedicated arm bath unit
skills.bnys_tip: For asthma management, hot arm bath at 42–44°C creates a counter-irritant and derivative effect by drawing blood to the periphery, reducing pulmonary congestion. Always combine with steam inhalation and diaphragmatic breathing exercises in the same session.
skills.india_note: In rural India, simple plastic washing tubs serve as effective arm bath vessels. Ensure tubs are dedicated to clinical use and disinfected between patients. Carry a verified thermometer as water temperature perception among patients can be unreliable.

skills.title: Foot Bath (Hot / Cold Contrast)
indication: Common cold and early influenza (hot foot bath)
Chronic insomnia (hot foot bath before sleep)
Tinea pedis and fungal infections (medicated cold)
Flat foot and plantar fasciitis rehabilitation
contraindication: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (risk of thermal injury)
Active deep vein thrombosis of lower limb
Open wounds or ulcers on foot or ankle
Severe varicose veins with skin changes
technique: Step 1: Prepare two foot bath basins — hot (40–44°C) and cold (15–18°C). For simple hot foot bath, use a single basin at 40–42°C, deep enough to submerge feet to mid-calf.
Step 2: Seat the patient with feet resting comfortably on a folded towel beside the basin; wrap the upper body with a blanket to promote diaphoresis in febrile conditions.
Step 3: Submerge feet gradually (toes first) in hot water; confirm patient comfort and monitor facial expression for signs of thermal discomfort.
Step 4: For contrast technique: 4 minutes hot → 1 minute cold → repeat 3–4 cycles. End on cold for stimulating effect or on warm for sedative/relaxation effect.
Step 5: Add mustard powder (10–15 g per 5 litres) to hot water for enhanced hyperaemic effect in cold/flu; add Epsom salt (50 g per 5 L) for oedema.
Step 6: Remove feet, dry thoroughly (especially between toes), and apply cotton socks immediately if patient is ambulatory.
skills.frequency: Once daily (hot); contrast foot bath alternate days or twice weekly
equipment: Two foot bath basins (plastic or stainless steel)
skills.bnys_tip: Hot mustard foot bath is one of the most effective and low-cost interventions for early-stage upper respiratory tract infections. The hyperaemic derivative effect decongests the nasal mucosa within 15–20 minutes. Educate patients to self-administer at home using kitchen mustard.
skills.india_note: Mustard seeds (rai/sarson) are universally available across India and are an economical, culturally familiar additive. During monsoon season, ensure basin water is not contaminated; use filtered or boiled-and-cooled water for immunocompromised patients.

skills.title: Hot Fomentation
indication: Chronic arthritis — joint stiffness and pain relief
Renal colic (abdominal fomentation for pain relief)
contraindication: Acute inflammatory phase (first 24–48 hours post-injury)
Malignancy in target area
Impaired skin sensation (risk of scalding)
Active bleeding or bruising at application site
technique: Step 1: Heat water to 60–70°C. Wring a thick Turkish towel or fomentation cloth tightly to remove excess water, using rubber gloves to prevent burns.
Step 2: Wrap the hot fomentation in a dry flannel cloth to buffer direct heat; test temperature on the inner wrist before applying to patient.
Step 3: Apply to the target area; place a dry insulating towel above the fomentation to retain heat. Patient should report comfortable warmth, not burning.
Step 4: After every 2 minutes, lift and check for erythema; replace the fomentation with a freshly wrung one as it cools.
Step 5: Apply cold compression (cold wet towel) for 30 seconds between each fomentation change to prevent passive congestion and stimulate circulation.
Step 6: Complete 3–4 fomentation cycles. End with a cold rub over the area; dry thoroughly and keep the part warm post-procedure.
skills.frequency: Once or twice daily
skills.bnys_tip: Always apply cold compress between fomentation changes — this critical step prevents venous stasis, which can paradoxically worsen pain and inflammation if omitted. The alternating hot-cold cycle is the therapeutic mechanism, not heat alone.
skills.india_note: Castor oil application over the fomentation area (Kaster tail patti) is a traditional Indian modification that enhances anti-inflammatory benefit in conditions like abdominal colic and arthritis; evidence supports its use as an adjunct in BNYS hospitals.

skills.title: Cold Wet Pack (Trunk / Local)
contraindication: Advanced arteriosclerosis
Active pulmonary tuberculosis (trunk pack)
Marked anaemia or debilitated states
Cardiac failure with peripheral oedema
technique: Step 1: Soak a cotton cloth (size appropriate: trunk pack = full torso width; local pack = 15×30 cm) in cold water (15–20°C) and wring to damp-dry consistency.
Step 2: Apply the damp cloth directly over the target area — trunk or local site.
Step 3: Cover immediately with a dry flannel or wool bandage to retain heat and stimulate a reactive hyperaemic response; seal edges to prevent cold air ingress.
Step 4: Leave in place; within 10–15 minutes the pack should warm to body temperature (if not, reinvestigate insulation or patient's vascular response).
Step 5: Change pack every 20–30 minutes for fever management; for chronic conditions, pack may be left up to 45 minutes.
Step 6: Remove pack, dry area briskly with a rough towel to stimulate circulation; advise rest for 15 minutes post-procedure.
skills.frequency: Once or twice daily (twice daily in acute fever)
equipment: Thin cotton cloth (muslin or unbleached cotton)
skills.bnys_tip: A cold wet pack that fails to warm within 15 minutes indicates poor peripheral circulation — document this as a vascular sign and consider contrast therapy instead. The therapeutic goal is reactive hyperaemia, not sustained cooling.
skills.india_note: Unbleached khadi cotton is the ideal material for cold wet packs due to its superior moisture retention and breathability; it is inexpensive and widely available at khadi stores across India. Avoid synthetic fabrics entirely.

skills.title: Wet Sheet Pack
indication: High fever — tepid wet sheet for rapid cooling
contraindication: Claustrophobia or agitated uncooperative patients
Cardiovascular insufficiency
Peripheral vascular disease with poor reactive response
Severe respiratory compromise (limits chest expansion)
technique: Step 1: Prepare a single cotton sheet (bed-size) soaked in cold water (15–22°C) and wrung firmly. Lay a waterproof sheet on the treatment table, then spread a dry wool blanket, then the wet sheet on top.
Step 2: Instruct patient to lie supine at the centre of the wet sheet with arms at sides. Rapidly wrap the wet sheet around the body from neck to feet, ensuring no air pockets.
Step 3: Wrap the dry wool blanket over the wet sheet, tucking edges firmly beneath the patient to seal heat in.

Step 4: Monitor in phases — Stage 1 (0–10 min): cool/tonic effect (shivering may occur, reassure patient); Stage 2 (10–20 min): neutral/sedative effect; Stage 3 (20–40 min): diaphoretic/heating effect with profuse sweating.
Step 5: For fever management, unwrap at Stage 1 completion; for de-addiction and skin conditions, allow progression to Stage 3.
Step 6: Unwrap, quickly dry the patient with a brisk towel rub, dress in dry clothing, provide warm herbal drink (ginger-tulsi water), and advise 30-minute rest.
skills.frequency: Daily in acute phase; 3 times per week in chronic management
skills.bnys_tip: Wet sheet pack is one of BNYS's most powerful systemic hydrotherapy tools. The transition through three physiological stages (tonic → sedative → diaphoretic) must be understood and communicated to the patient before the procedure to ensure compliance and prevent anxiety.
skills.india_note: In Indian naturopathic hospitals, the wet sheet pack is frequently used in de-addiction programmes (alcohol, tobacco) where diaphoresis aids in toxin elimination. Tulsi-ginger decoction post-procedure is a culturally accepted warm drink that supports recovery and improves patient experience.

skills.title: Immersion Bath (Full Tub Bath)
indication: Sports recovery — cold plunge or contrast immersion
contraindication: Active cardiac failure or uncompensated valvular disease
Open wounds or communicable skin infections
Epilepsy (unsupervised; supervised immersion acceptable)
Severe hypotension — cold immersion contraindicated
technique: Step 1: Fill tub to three-quarter capacity at prescribed temperature: cold plunge (10–18°C), tepid (30–33°C), neutral (34–36°C), hot (37–40°C). Verify with calibrated thermometer.
Step 2: Assist patient into the tub slowly, feet-first; ensure the patient is stable and seated/reclining safely without slipping (use non-slip mat in tub).
Step 3: Submerge the body to shoulder level; support the neck with a waterproof pillow. Keep the head above water at all times unless specifically performing head bath.
Step 4: Maintain temperature by adding controlled amounts of hot or cold water; monitor patient's pulse rate and skin colour every 5 minutes.
Step 5: For salt bath (skin conditions), dissolve 500 g Epsom salt per 100 L water before entry; for herbal immersion, add neem leaf decoction or dilute essential oils.
Step 6: Assist patient out carefully to prevent slipping and orthostatic hypotension; dry quickly, dress in warm clothing, and document temperature, HR, and patient response.
skills.frequency: Once daily or as clinically indicated; cold plunge post-exercise as needed
equipment: Stainless steel jug for controlled water addition
skills.bnys_tip: Neutral immersion bath (34–36°C) for 20 minutes is the closest hydrotherapy equivalent to a sedative — it reduces sympathetic tone, lowers cortisol, and is the intervention of choice in patients presenting with anxiety-related insomnia. Combine with lavender-oil addition (3–5 drops) for enhanced relaxation.
skills.india_note: Neem (Azadirachta indica) leaf decoction added to immersion baths is widely practiced in Indian naturopathic hospitals for skin conditions. Boil 100 g fresh neem leaves in 2 litres of water, filter, and add to tub. This aligns with Ayurvedic practice and is well-accepted by Indian patients.

skills.title: Mud Pack (Abdominal / Local)
indication: Skin conditions: acne, furuncles, local inflammation
contraindication: Active skin infection or open wounds at application site
Allergy to mud or clay minerals
Abdominal pack contraindicated in pregnancy
Active peptic ulcer (abdominal variant)
technique: Step 1: Prepare therapeutic mud by soaking clean, sun-dried mud (black cotton soil or Fuller's earth) in clean water 8–12 hours prior; consistency should be thick paste (not runny). Ensure no stones or foreign matter.
Step 2: Apply mud at room temperature (25–30°C) directly on a clean muslin cloth cut to appropriate size — abdominal pack (30×40 cm), local pack (15×20 cm).
Step 3: Lay patient supine (for abdominal pack); apply mud-laden cloth directly over the skin of the target area with mud thickness approximately 1.5–2 cm.
Step 4: Cover with a dry flannel cloth or plastic sheet to prevent mess and slow drying. For local joint packs, bandage lightly in place.
Step 5: Leave in position for prescribed time; observe patient for adverse skin reactions (rare). Mud will dry and cool progressively, providing a drawing and cooling effect.
Step 6: Remove mud pack, wipe area clean with a damp cloth, dry thoroughly. Advise patient to avoid cold exposure to the treated area for 30 minutes.
skills.frequency: Once daily; local packs may be applied twice daily in acute inflammation
equipment: Basin of clean water for preparation
skills.bnys_tip: The abdominal mud pack's primary action is through its hygroscopic cooling effect — it reduces abdominal sympathetic tone and promotes parasympathetic activity, enhancing peristalsis. Apply consistently in the morning on an empty stomach for best results in constipation management.
skills.india_note: Black cotton soil (regur) from the Deccan Plateau region of Maharashtra and Karnataka is the most therapeutically preferred mud in Indian naturopathy due to its high mineral content and hygroscopic properties. Ensure mud is sourced from unpolluted, non-agricultural areas free from pesticide contamination.

skills.title: Full Mud Bath
indication: Rheumatoid arthritis — full body joint involvement
contraindication: Claustrophobia or severe anxiety disorders (may worsen)
Active febrile illness
Open wounds, extensive skin lesions, or oozing dermatitis
Uncontrolled hypertension or cardiac failure
technique: Step 1: Prepare a full mud bath tub by mixing screened therapeutic mud with warm water (30–35°C) to semi-fluid consistency; fill tub to a depth of 20–25 cm (sufficient to submerge lying patient).
Step 2: Assist patient (clad in swimwear) into the tub; spread mud evenly over the full body surface including scalp (optional) and face (optional, with eyes protected).
Step 3: Ensure patient is comfortable in semi-reclining position; place a dry towel roll under the head to keep the face clear of mud.
Step 4: Monitor patient at 5-minute intervals for comfort, temperature tolerance, and any signs of vasovagal response.
Step 5: At completion, assist patient out of tub onto a plastic-covered table; remove bulk mud with wooden spatula, then transfer patient directly to shower.
Step 6: Patient showers with warm water to remove all mud residue; dry thoroughly, dress warmly, and rest for 30 minutes. Offer warm water or herbal drink.
skills.frequency: Every alternate day; course of 7–10 sessions
equipment: Full body mud bath tub (dedicated, minimum 180×70 cm)
skills.bnys_tip: Full mud bath is uniquely effective in psoriasis — the mineral content (silica, magnesium, iron oxides) combined with the occlusive effect mimics Dead Sea therapy. Document PASI score before and after a 10-session course to track objective improvement.
skills.india_note: Several established naturopathic hospitals in India (including those in Pune, Bangalore, and Hyderabad) have purpose-built mud bath facilities. For hospitals without dedicated tubs, a tiled outdoor mud bath area exposed to sunlight is functionally equivalent and may enhance the therapeutic effect through heliotherapy.

skills.title: Mud Face Pack
indication: Enlarged pores and skin texture irregularities
contraindication: Active eczema or contact dermatitis on face
Known allergy to clay or mineral pigments
Fresh facial wounds, burns, or post-procedure skin
Rosacea (may trigger flare in sensitive phenotypes)
technique: Step 1: Prepare mud paste to smooth, lump-free consistency using filtered water; add 1 teaspoon neem powder or turmeric to enhance antibacterial effect for acne.
Step 2: Cleanse patient's face with plain warm water and pat dry. Tie hair back securely; place a head wrap to protect hairline.
Step 3: Apply mud paste evenly over face using a spatula or gloved fingers, avoiding the periorbital area, nostrils, and lips; thickness approximately 3–5 mm.
Step 4: Leave to dry; as it dries the pack will draw excess sebum and surface toxins, creating a mild tightening sensation — inform patient this is expected.
Step 5: Once partially dry (not fully hardened), remove by dampening with a warm wet sponge and wiping off in upward strokes to avoid dragging skin downward.
Step 6: Rinse with cool water, pat dry, and apply rose water or aloe vera gel to soothe. Advise avoiding sun exposure for 2 hours post-application.
skills.frequency: Twice weekly for acne; once weekly for maintenance
equipment: Therapeutic mud (Fuller's earth or multani mitti)
Rose water or aloe vera gel
skills.bnys_tip: Multani mitti (Fuller's earth) is the most clinically effective mud for the face — its high absorptive capacity makes it superior for oily and acne-prone skin. Adding a few drops of tea tree oil to the paste provides enhanced antimicrobial action without the adverse effects of topical antibiotics.
skills.india_note: Multani mitti is sold in every kirana store across India and is the most cost-accessible natural cosmetic clay available. Patients can be taught self-application at home as part of a naturopathic skin care prescription, making it an excellent tool for extending clinical benefit between visits.

skills.title: Mud Eye Pack
indication: Eye strain and computer vision syndrome
contraindication: Active infectious conjunctivitis or keratitis
Recent intraocular surgery or corneal injury
Glaucoma (avoid pressure on globe)
Mud allergy or periorbital contact dermatitis
technique: Step 1: Prepare clean, screened mud (preferably Fuller's earth or white clay) to a smooth paste consistency using chilled or room-temperature filtered water.
Step 2: Ask patient to lie supine comfortably; instruct them to close eyes gently — do not press lids shut under pressure.
Step 3: Apply mud paste carefully over closed eyelids only (upper and lower); keep paste thickness to 3–4 mm; never allow mud to enter the conjunctival sac or perinasal area.
Step 4: Place a thin muslin cloth over the mud pack to stabilise it and prevent dripping; patient should remain still and in a relaxed state.
Step 5: Remove after prescribed time by dampening gently with a clean wet cotton pad; wipe from inner to outer canthus to prevent entry into tear duct.
Step 6: Clean area with cool water and cotton pads; apply diluted rose water (1:1) using a cotton pad as a soothing final rinse.
skills.frequency: Daily during an acute course; twice weekly for chronic eye strain
skills.bnys_tip: Use chilled (not ice cold) filtered water to mix mud for eye packs — the cooling effect is a significant part of the therapeutic mechanism for eye strain and periorbital oedema. The mud should feel pleasantly cool on the eyelid, not uncomfortable.
skills.india_note: This treatment is highly popular at naturopathic wellness centres in Indian cities, particularly for software professionals with severe digital eye strain. Patient education about the 20-20-20 rule should accompany the procedure as part of a holistic naturopathic prescription.

skills.title: Mud Spine Pack
indication: Lumbar spondylosis and mechanical low back pain
Spinal muscular fatigue and paravertebral tension
contraindication: Spinal fracture or recent spinal surgery
Cauda equina syndrome (emergent surgical condition)
Active spinal infections (Pott's disease)
Open wounds or pressure ulcers on spinal area
technique: Step 1: Prepare a strip of mud-laden muslin cloth (approximately 10–12 cm wide × full spinal length) using therapeutic mud at room temperature or slightly warm (30–32°C).
Step 2: Patient lies prone on a clean, covered treatment table with a pillow under the ankles to relax lumbar lordosis.
Step 3: Apply the mud cloth centrally along the vertebral column from the cervico-dorsal junction to the sacrum, ensuring even coverage over all spinous processes.
Step 4: Cover with a dry flannel strip and a thin waterproof layer to retain temperature and prevent mess.
Step 5: Monitor patient comfort; mud will progressively cool and draw — this gentle cooling along the sympathetic chain contributes to the vasodilatory and analgesic effect.
Step 6: Remove pack, clean area with damp warm towels; follow immediately with a 10-minute hot fomentation along the paravertebral muscles, then a brief cold friction rub.
skills.frequency: Daily or on alternate days; course of 7–14 sessions
equipment: Long muslin cloth strip (10 cm × 90 cm)
skills.bnys_tip: The combination of mud spine pack followed by spinal bath is the cornerstone protocol for lumbar spondylosis in BNYS inpatient settings. Document VAS pain scores and lumbar flexion range before and after each week of treatment to objectively assess progress.
skills.india_note: In BNYS colleges across South India (especially Karnataka and Tamil Nadu), the mud spine pack is taught as a first-line treatment for IT professionals presenting with occupational low back pain. Combining this with a prescribed ergonomic workstation assessment is culturally relevant for this patient demographic.

skills.title: Swedish Massage — Basic Five Techniques
contraindication: Active inflammatory phase of injury (first 48–72 hours)
Deep vein thrombosis — any limb
Active skin infection, eczema, or open lesions on treatment area
Uncontrolled hypertension or cardiac arrhythmia
technique: Step 1 — Effleurage: Begin with long, gliding strokes using palmar surface of both hands; apply centripetal direction (toward the heart) with even, rhythmic pressure (2–3 kg/cm²). Used to warm tissue, spread lubricant, and assess tissue tone.

Step 2 — Petrissage: Kneading technique using palms and fingers; pick up, roll, and compress muscle bellies between thumb and fingers. Applied to large muscle groups (trapezius, quadriceps, gluteals) to improve venous return and release myofascial adhesions.

Step 3 — Tapotement: Rhythmic percussion using cupped hands, fingertips, or ulnar border of fists. Applied to thoracic and lumbar areas; contraindicated over bony prominences and kidneys. Used for stimulation and airway clearance in respiratory patients.

Step 4 — Friction: Deep circular or transverse pressure applied with fingertip or thumb pad over specific trigger points, scar tissue, or tendon insertions. Applied perpendicular to muscle fibre direction to break adhesions.

Step 5 — Vibration: Fine oscillating movements applied through the practitioner's hands; used over nerve pathways and reflex zones to produce sedative or stimulating effect depending on speed and depth.
skills.frequency: Two or three times per week; daily in intensive inpatient therapy
skills.bnys_tip: In BNYS practice, effleurage is not merely introductory — it is diagnostic. Assess tissue temperature, turgor, and superficial adhesions during the initial effleurage strokes before proceeding to deeper techniques. This sets the clinical intention of the massage, differentiating BNYS massage from recreational spa work.
skills.india_note: Cold-pressed sesame oil (til ka tel) is the preferred massage medium in Indian naturopathy due to its anecdotally validated anti-inflammatory properties and skin nourishment. Warm the oil to 35–38°C before application, particularly in winter months in North India, to enhance vasodilatory effect and patient comfort.

skills.title: Therapeutic Abdominal Massage
contraindication: Acute abdomen — appendicitis, peritonitis, visceral perforation
Active peptic ulcer disease with recent bleed
Pregnancy (especially first trimester — deep massage contraindicated)
Post-abdominal surgery (within 6 weeks)
technique: Step 1: Patient lies supine with knees slightly flexed (pillow under knees) to relax abdominal wall musculature. Warm hands with oil; begin with light effleurage over the full abdomen in clockwise direction.

Step 2: Colon massage sequence — begin at the right iliac fossa (ascending colon), stroke upward to right hypochondrium, across the transverse colon to left hypochondrium, and down to left iliac fossa (sigmoid). This follows anatomical direction of peristalsis.
Step 3: Apply petrissage to the lateral abdominal walls (oblique muscles); use a gentle kneading technique bilaterally to release tension in the abdominal wall.
Step 4: Apply deeper effleurage pressure over the sigmoid colon area using two-finger technique or heel of palm; assess for palpable faecal loading and document.
Step 5: Perform vibration technique over the caecal and hepatic flexure areas to stimulate local peristalsis and ileocaecal valve function.
Step 6: Complete with 5–10 large clockwise effleurage strokes to consolidate the peristaltic wave direction; instruct patient to lie for 10 minutes before ambulating.
skills.frequency: Daily in constipation management; 3 times per week for IBS maintenance
skills.bnys_tip: The colon massage sequence — ascending → transverse → descending → sigmoid — must always follow anatomical peristaltic direction. Reverse massage direction (anti-clockwise) is contraindicated in constipation as it actively opposes faecal transit. This is a common error in inexperienced practitioners.
skills.india_note: Castor oil (arandi ka tel) warm abdominal massage is a traditional Indian practice for constipation that has evidence support for improving intestinal transit time. Recommend patients self-administer a simplified clockwise castor oil abdominal massage each morning before arising from bed.

skills.title: Head Massage — Shiro Abhyanga
contraindication: Active scalp infection (tinea capitis, furuncles)
Recent head or neck surgery
Raised intracranial pressure
Active migraine attack (massage during acute attack may worsen; indicated inter-ictally)
technique: Step 1: Patient seated comfortably in a chair with back support; practitioner stands behind. Warm 20–30 mL of sesame or brahmi oil to body temperature.
Step 2: Apply oil to scalp using fingertips; distribute evenly from frontal to occipital area. Begin with light effleurage strokes across the scalp using palmar surface.
Step 3: Scalp massage — using fingertip pads of both hands, apply deep circular friction movements across the entire scalp; focus on temporal, parietal, and occipital zones for 5 minutes.

Step 4: Neck and suboccipital massage — apply effleurage and petrissage strokes from shoulders up to occiput; specific attention to suboccipital muscles (rectus capitis, oblique capitis) using bilateral thumb friction.
Step 5: Perform tapotement (finger-tip hacking) gently over the vertex and bilateral temporal regions for 2–3 minutes to stimulate peripheral circulation.
Step 6: Complete with slow, firm effleurage strokes from forehead to occiput; apply slight traction at the occiput with cupped hands to decompress suboccipital articulation gently.
skills.frequency: Daily in acute phase; twice weekly for maintenance
equipment: Brahmi oil or warm sesame oil
skills.bnys_tip: Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) oil is the preferred medium for scalp massage in BNYS practice for neurological indications — its active bacosides have evidence for anxiolytic and neuroprotective effects at the scalp absorption level. For hair loss, bhringraj (Eclipta alba) oil is the first-line recommendation.
skills.india_note: Shiro Abhyanga has deep cultural roots in India and is widely accepted by patients across all socioeconomic strata. Leveraging this cultural familiarity improves patient compliance with a regular home self-massage protocol, which is a key component of naturopathic treatment for chronic headache and insomnia management.

skills.title: Foot Reflexology Pressure Technique
indication: Plantar fasciitis rehabilitation and general foot health
contraindication: Foot fractures, recent foot surgery, or DVT
Severe peripheral neuropathy (sensory impairment)
Active foot infection or open lesions
First trimester pregnancy (certain reflex zones contraindicated)
technique: Step 1: Patient supine, feet at the practitioner's waist level on a firm surface. Cleanse feet with warm water, dry thoroughly. Apply light talcum powder or unscented oil to facilitate smooth movement.
Step 2: Begin with relaxation techniques — rotate ankles in both directions (5 rotations each), flex and extend toes, apply firm effleurage over dorsal and plantar surfaces.
Step 3: Thumb-walking technique — using the medial edge of the flexed thumb, apply rhythmic caterpillar-like walking movements across the plantar surface in systematic zones from heel to toe.

Step 4: Identify and work organ reflex zones: solar plexus point (centre of foot — circular pressure for relaxation), intestinal zone (arch area), spine zone (medial border of foot), head/sinus zone (toes).
Step 5: Apply sustained 3–5 second pressure to identified tender reflex points; tenderness at a reflex zone is considered diagnostically significant in reflexology — document for clinical tracking.
Step 6: Complete with relaxation strokes and passive toe articulation. Advise patient to drink 2 glasses of water post-session to facilitate toxin elimination.
skills.frequency: Twice to three times per week
equipment: Warm water and towels for foot cleansing
skills.bnys_tip: Reflexology is most effective when integrated with other BNYS treatments, not used in isolation. The post-session water prescription (500 mL within 30 minutes) is not merely ritual — it supports renal clearance of metabolic byproducts mobilised during the treatment.
skills.india_note: Reflexology charts used in Indian BNYS colleges are often based on Eunice Ingham's original maps; supplement with Traditional Chinese Medicine foot maps for comparing zone locations. Cross-referencing Ayurvedic marma points with reflexology zones creates a uniquely Indian integrative approach that resonates with patients.

skills.title: Sports Massage Basics
indication: Pre-event preparation — muscle activation and circulation
Overuse injuries — shin splints, tennis elbow
contraindication: Acute soft tissue injury within 48 hours (RICE protocol phase)
Suspected fracture or dislocation
Compartment syndrome
Active skin infection over treatment area
technique: Step 1 — Pre-event massage (15 min): Brisk, stimulating effleurage (faster rhythm than Swedish), tapotement over major muscle groups (quadriceps, hamstrings, gastrocnemius). Do NOT perform deep tissue work — risk of impairing performance.
Step 2 — Post-event massage (30–45 min): Begin after a 30-minute cool-down period. Start with light effleurage to assess tissue status; patient may present with DOMS, cramping, or oedema.

Step 3 — Compression technique: Apply direct manual compression over muscle belly using palmar heel; hold for 3–5 seconds, release, repeat along muscle length. Particularly effective for quadriceps and hamstrings.
Step 4 — Cross-fibre friction: Applied at the musculotendinous junction and over tendon insertions at 90° to fibre direction; used for breaking adhesions in overuse injuries.

Step 5 — Muscle energy technique integration: Combine passive stretching with the massage — have the patient perform gentle isometric contraction followed by relaxation (PNF principle) while the practitioner simultaneously stretches the muscle.
Step 6 — Complete with ice massage or cold compress over the most affected areas post-massage to manage any reactive oedema; document pre- and post-ROM measurements.
skills.frequency: Pre-event: morning of event; Post-event: within 2–4 hours post-exertion; maintenance: weekly
equipment: Sports massage oil (arnica-based or camphor oil)
skills.bnys_tip: Do not perform deep sports massage within 6 hours pre-event — this is a critical clinical error. Pre-event massage should be exclusively stimulating and superficial; deep work increases tissue laxity and can reduce proprioceptive performance, increasing injury risk.
skills.india_note: Camphor oil (kapur tel) diluted in sesame oil is widely used in Indian sports massage, particularly in traditional akhadas (wrestling training centres) and cricket training camps. Its counter-irritant properties provide subjective pain relief and are culturally familiar to Indian athletes.

skills.title: Therapeutic Fasting — Preparation and Monitoring
indication: Gastrointestinal cleansing and functional bowel disorders
contraindication: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (absolute contraindication)
Cardiac cachexia or severe undernutrition (BMI < 17.5)
Active peptic ulcer disease
Pregnant or lactating women
technique: Step 1 — Pre-fast assessment: Document BMI, blood pressure, fasting blood glucose, renal function (urea, creatinine), complete blood count. Assess psychological readiness and social support. Prescribe 3-day transition diet (gradual reduction in solid foods, increase in fruits and vegetables).

Step 2 — Type selection: Prescribe fasting type based on clinical status — Water fast (clinical supervision only); Juice fast (citrus/vegetable juices 200–300 mL every 3 hours); Fruit fast (mono-fruit or mixed); Raw food fast.

Step 3 — Monitoring during fast: Daily vitals (BP, pulse, weight, temperature); blood glucose monitoring twice daily in diabetics; urine ketone testing on day 3+; document subjective symptoms (headache, nausea, weakness).
Step 4 — Adjunct therapy during fast: Maintain daily enema (plain water or neem water) to clear bowel; continue yoga and pranayama (mild); maintain adequate hydration (2–3 L water/day).

Step 5 — Breaking the fast: This is the most critical phase. Begin with diluted fresh fruit juice (50%) on Day 1 break; transition to fresh fruits on Day 2; add cooked vegetables on Day 3; reintroduce cereals on Day 4–5. Breaking a fast improperly causes more harm than the fast itself.

Step 6 — Post-fast dietary prescription: Provide written dietary plan with caloric and macronutrient targets; schedule follow-up within 48 hours of completing the fast for weight and clinical parameter reassessment.
skills.duration: 3–7 days (juice/fruit fast); 1–3 days (water fast under supervision only)
skills.frequency: Once per quarter for maintenance; as clinically indicated for acute conditions
skills.bnys_tip: The fasting crisis (Heilungskrise or healing crisis) — characterised by temporary worsening of symptoms, headache, and fatigue on days 2–3 — is a recognised naturopathic phenomenon reflecting rapid autolysis. Counsel patients about this before the fast begins to prevent premature discontinuation.
skills.india_note: Religious fasting (Ekadashi, Navratri, Ramzan) is culturally normalised in India and provides a unique patient entry point for introducing therapeutic fasting. Leverage this cultural familiarity, but clarify the clinical distinctions — therapeutic fasting requires monitored gradual refeeding, unlike religious fasts which typically end abruptly with heavy meals.

skills.title: Mono Diet Prescription
indication: Elimination phase in food intolerance workup
contraindication: Severe malnutrition or eating disorders
Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus
Known allergy to the prescribed mono-food
Prolonged use (> 10 days) without clinical supervision
technique: Step 1 — Food selection: Choose a mono food based on clinical indication — Grapes (diuretic, detox), Banana (gut soothing, IBS), Apple (pectin for bowel regulation), Watermelon (renal cleanse, hypertension), Coconut water (electrolyte repletion), Brown rice (gut rest).

Step 2 — Quantity prescription: Calculate energy intake to ensure minimum 800–1000 kcal/day (minimum adequate mono diet). Instruct on meal frequency — 5–6 small servings throughout the day rather than 3 large meals.
Step 3 — Duration and monitoring: 3–7 days typical duration. Monitor body weight daily (acceptable loss: 0.3–0.5 kg/day); assess energy levels, bowel frequency, and skin changes.
Step 4 — Hydration protocol: Ensure 2.5–3 L water per day in addition to the mono food; document urine output and colour (target: pale yellow).
Step 5 — Transition protocol: After mono diet, introduce one new food per day in a graduated fashion; maintain food symptom diary to identify reactions.
Step 6 — Documentation: Record mono diet type, duration, daily food quantity consumed, clinical observations, and transition foods for the medical record.
skills.frequency: Monthly or as clinically indicated; short 1–3 day courses as seasonal maintenance
equipment: Nutritional reference chart for the mono food
skills.bnys_tip: The grape cure (Uvam therapy) is a classical naturopathic mono diet with specific clinical protocols developed by Johanna Brandt. In the BNYS context, prescribe black grapes (including seeds and skin) 1.5–2 kg per day as both food and medicine — grape polyphenols provide antioxidant support during the detoxification phase.
skills.india_note: Seasonal availability profoundly affects mono diet prescription in India. Watermelon mono diet is most practical in April–June across most of India; grape therapy is best prescribed in October–January when Nashik and Bijapur grapes are at peak nutrition and minimum cost. Align prescription with seasonality (Ritucharya principle).

skills.title: Elimination Diet Protocol
indication: Suspected food intolerance or non-IgE food sensitivity
Chronic urticaria with suspected dietary trigger
contraindication: Anorexia nervosa or orthorexia nervosa
IgE-mediated food allergy requiring formal allergist workup
Pregnant women without specialist dietitian supervision
Patients with rigid dietary restrictions unable to adhere to protocol
technique: Step 1 — Pre-elimination assessment: Document current symptoms using validated scales (IBS-SSS for bowel symptoms, SCORAD for eczema, or headache diary). Record all foods consumed in a 7-day food diary; identify top suspected triggers.

Step 2 — Elimination phase (2–4 weeks): Remove all common allergens/irritants simultaneously — gluten-containing grains, dairy, eggs, soy, tree nuts, peanuts, shellfish, artificial additives, caffeine, and alcohol. Prescribe allowed foods list in writing.
Step 3 — Symptom monitoring: Use daily symptom diary during elimination phase; expect possible worsening in week 1 (withdrawal effect), followed by improvement from week 2 onward.

Step 4 — Reintroduction phase: Reintroduce one eliminated food every 72 hours; instruct patient to consume the food in a significant quantity (2–3 servings on challenge day) to elicit a response if present.
Step 5 — Reaction documentation: Record symptoms (GI, skin, energy, headache) within 0–72 hours post-reintroduction; positive reactions indicate the food as a likely trigger.
Step 6 — Finalise personalised diet plan: Based on reintroduction results, prescribe a written long-term dietary plan that excludes confirmed triggers while ensuring nutritional completeness.
skills.duration: 6–8 weeks total (2–4 weeks elimination + 4 weeks reintroduction)
skills.frequency: One complete protocol; follow-up monthly post-protocol
equipment: Daily food and symptom diary template
Patient education handout on reading food labels
skills.bnys_tip: The most common BNYS elimination diet error is prescribing the elimination phase without the structured reintroduction phase. Without systematic reintroduction, the protocol has no diagnostic value and patients remain on unnecessarily restricted diets indefinitely — this has nutritional and psychological consequences.
skills.india_note: In the Indian context, wheat and dairy elimination can be culturally disruptive (chapati and chai are dietary staples). Prescribe rice-based alternatives for wheat and coconut milk or plant-based preparations for dairy to ensure cultural dietary adherence. Work with patients' families where possible, as food preparation in India is largely communal.

skills.title: Juice Therapy Prescription
indication: Digestive rest and mucosal healing (gastritis, IBD)
Anaemia — green juice and beetroot therapy
Hepatic support — bitter gourd and carrot
contraindication: Diabetes mellitus (high-fructose fruit juices contraindicated; vegetable juices monitored)
Chronic kidney disease — high potassium juices (spinach, carrot) contraindicated
Haemochromatosis — avoid excessive iron-rich green juices
Patients on warfarin — consistent green vegetable juice alters INR
technique: Step 1 — Clinical diagnosis-based selection: Match juice to clinical indication — Beetroot + carrot (anaemia, liver); Ash gourd/ridge gourd (cooling, UTI); Amla (antioxidant, immunity); Bottle gourd (cardiac, hypertension); Wheatgrass (detox, haemoglobin); Bitter gourd (diabetes adjunct).

Step 2 — Preparation standards: All produce must be organically grown or thoroughly washed in potassium permanganate solution (1:10,000). Use cold-press juicer where available to preserve enzyme and nutrient content; centrifugal juicers are acceptable.

Step 3 — Quantity and timing: Prescribe 150–200 mL per dose, 2–3 times daily. Consume fresh within 15–20 minutes of extraction; refrigerated storage acceptable up to 4 hours only. Consume on empty stomach (30 minutes before meals).

Step 4 — Combination rules: Do not combine fruit and vegetable juices in the same prescription serving. Citrus juices are not combined with milk or starchy foods. Green vegetable juices can be combined with lemon juice (10 mL) for palatability.
Step 5 — Monitoring: Track body weight, energy levels, and specific clinical parameters (BP in hypertension, HbA1c at 3 months in diabetes, Hb at 4 weeks in anaemia).
Step 6 — Duration and transition: 7–14 day juice therapy courses followed by a balanced whole food diet. Provide a written juice prescription card with specific recipes, timing, and quantities.
skills.duration: 150–200 mL per serving, 2–3 servings per day
skills.frequency: Daily for prescribed course duration; maintenance 1 serving per day ongoing
skills.bnys_tip: Wheatgrass juice (30 mL on an empty stomach) is the most potent concentrated juice in the BNYS pharmacopoeia — it contains chlorophyll (structural analogue of haemoglobin), vitamins, and enzymes. Counsel patients about the expected nausea on initial days (tolerance develops by day 4–5) to prevent premature discontinuation.
skills.india_note: Amla (Indian Gooseberry) juice is one of the most evidence-backed naturopathic juices available in India — it has demonstrated effects on lipid profiles, blood glucose, and immunity in multiple Indian clinical trials. Fresh amla is seasonally available October–February; dried amla or amla churna can substitute.

skills.title: Postural Assessment (Anterior / Lateral / Posterior)
indication: Pre- and post-yoga therapy outcome measurement
contraindication: Acute pain preventing comfortable standing
Vestibular dysfunction preventing standing assessment (perform seated)
Recent lower limb surgery (modify assessment protocol)
Severe cognitive impairment affecting instruction compliance
technique: Step 1 — Preparation: Patient in minimal clothing (shorts/sports bra); standing barefoot on a flat, hard surface. Use a plumb line or posture grid (available as a printed wall chart) as reference. Assess in three views: anterior, posterior, and lateral.

Step 2 — Anterior view assessment: Note head position (forward/tilted/rotated), shoulder height symmetry, clavicle symmetry, ASIS (anterior superior iliac spine) height comparison, patella orientation, foot arch (flat, normal, high), toe alignment.

Step 3 — Lateral view assessment: Ideal alignment — ear lobe directly above acromion, acromion above greater trochanter, greater trochanter above lateral knee, lateral knee above lateral malleolus. Assess cervical, thoracic, lumbar curvatures; note forward head posture measurement (distance from tragus to vertical reference).

Step 4 — Posterior view assessment: Occipital alignment, paravertebral muscle symmetry, scapular position (winging, elevation, protraction), spinal scoliosis (Adam's forward bend test if scoliosis suspected), gluteal fold symmetry, subtalar alignment (valgus/varus).
Step 5 — Documentation: Record findings on a standardised postural assessment form with body diagram. Photograph in all three planes with patient's written consent for longitudinal comparison.

Step 6 — Clinical correlation: Map identified postural deviations to probable muscle imbalances (e.g., anterior pelvic tilt → tight hip flexors, weak gluteals/abdominals); prescribe corrective asana programme accordingly.
skills.frequency: Initial assessment, then reassessment at 4 and 8 weeks
equipment: Tape measure (for forward head posture measurement)
Adam's forward bend test protocol sheet
skills.bnys_tip: Always perform postural assessment before prescribing asanas for musculoskeletal conditions — prescribing asanas without postural data is akin to prescribing medication without a clinical examination. Anterior pelvic tilt, the most prevalent postural deviation in Indian office workers, requires specific contraindication of postures that further anteriorly tilt the pelvis (deep backbends).
skills.india_note: India has a high prevalence of flat feet (pes planus) in urban populations, linked to early transition from barefoot walking to footwear and prolonged floor sitting. Foot arch assessment should be a standard component of every postural assessment in the Indian clinical context, as it significantly influences spinal alignment.

skills.title: Breathing Pattern Assessment (Diaphragmatic Check)
contraindication: Acute respiratory distress (assess after stabilisation)
Acute rib fracture (modified assessment only)
Severe claustrophobia (rebreathing assessment)
Pneumothorax (assessment of breathing effort may worsen)
technique: Step 1 — Resting breathing assessment: Patient supine or seated; observe breathing rate (count for 60 seconds — normal: 12–16/min), rhythm, and effort without informing the patient that breathing is being observed (to avoid voluntary modification).

Step 2 — Diaphragmatic vs apical assessment: Place one hand on the patient's abdomen (umbilical level) and one on the upper chest. Observe movement — dominant abdominal movement = diaphragmatic breathing (healthy); dominant chest movement = apical/clavicular breathing (dysfunctional pattern).

Step 3 — Breathing ratio: Time the inspiratory and expiratory phases using a stopwatch. Normal I:E ratio is 1:2. A reversed ratio (longer inspiration) indicates thoracic restriction; equal I:E ratio suggests anxiety or habitual hyperventilation.

Step 4 — Lateral chest expansion: Using a tape measure around the lower thorax (nipple level), measure circumference at full exhalation vs full inhalation. Normal expansion: 4–8 cm. Reduced expansion indicates restrictive pattern.
Step 5 — Nijhuis Three-Zone Palpation: Palpate in sequence — upper chest, mid-thorax, and abdomen — to identify the zone of dominant movement and zones of restriction.

Step 6 — Documentation and prescription: Record respiratory rate, I:E ratio, diaphragmatic dominance (yes/no), and lateral expansion; prescribe appropriate pranayama techniques (Vibhaagi pranayama, diaphragmatic retraining) based on findings.
skills.frequency: Initial assessment, then reassessment at 4 weeks and 8 weeks
skills.bnys_tip: The most important clinical finding in breathing assessment for a BNYS practitioner is the identification of upper-chest dominant breathing — this pattern is pathophysiologically linked to chronic anxiety, pain sensitisation, and cervical tension headaches. Correcting this with Vibhaagi (sectional) pranayama is often more effective than anxiolytic medication for mild-moderate anxiety.
skills.india_note: Pollution-related respiratory compromise is a significant issue in Indian metros (Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata) — assess for environmental trigger history in every breathing assessment. Co-prescribe Jalneti (nasal irrigation) and steam inhalation with eucalyptus or turmeric as standard adjuncts for urban patients with respiratory conditions.

skills.title: Flexibility and ROM Assessment
indication: Monitoring therapeutic response to asana therapy
contraindication: Acute joint inflammation — defer ROM testing until subacute
Recent fracture or joint replacement (protocol modification required)
Ligamentous instability (hypermobility testing requires caution)
Severe pain preventing full assessment — document limitation
technique: Step 1 — Spinal flexibility: Modified Schober test (lumbar flexion) — mark S1 and a point 10 cm above; measure with maximal forward flexion (normal: < 15 cm increase = hypomobility). Assess cervical ROM with goniometer: flexion (0–45°), extension (0–45°), lateral flexion (0–45° bilateral), rotation (0–60° bilateral).

Step 2 — Hip flexibility: Thomas test for hip flexor tightness; SLR (Straight Leg Raise) for hamstring flexibility (normal: > 70°); Patrick/FABER test for hip external rotation and sacroiliac joint mobility.

Step 3 — Shoulder ROM: Assess with goniometer — flexion (0–180°), extension (0–60°), abduction (0–180°), internal and external rotation (0–90° each). Note any painful arc (60–120° abduction = rotator cuff pathology).
Step 4 — Functional mobility tests: Sit-and-reach test (hamstring and lumbar flexibility); Overhead reach test (thoracic and shoulder); Wall angel test (thoracic mobility and scapular control).

Step 5 — Hypermobility screening: Beighton Hypermobility Score (9-point scale). Identify hypermobile patients (score > 4) as they require a different asana prescription — stabilising postures rather than further stretching.

Step 6 — Documentation: Record all ROM values in degrees using standardised forms; compare to age-sex normative data; identify short and long muscles; formulate asana prescription addressing identified imbalances.
skills.frequency: Initial, then reassessment at 6 and 12 weeks
equipment: ROM documentation form with normative values
skills.bnys_tip: Always identify hypermobility before prescribing asanas — hypermobile patients (Beighton score > 4) are frequently misidentified as 'very flexible and well-suited for yoga,' when they actually need strength and stabilisation-focused asanas (e.g., Tadasana with joint stacking, Setu Bandhasana) rather than further flexibility work.
skills.india_note: Female patients in India have a higher prevalence of generalised joint hypermobility (GJH) compared to Western populations, particularly in younger age groups. This is clinically relevant in BNYS yoga therapy classes — screen all female patients for hypermobility before group asana prescription to individualise their programme.

skills.title: Basic Acupressure Pressure Technique
indication: Nausea and vomiting — chemotherapy or motion sickness
contraindication: Pregnancy — specific points (SP6, LI4, GB21) strictly contraindicated
Local skin lesions, fracture, or infection over acupressure point
Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy (deep pressure contraindicated)
Cardiac pacemaker (electromagnetic acupressure devices)
technique: Step 1 — Point location: Identify the point accurately using anatomical landmarks and cun measurements (1 cun = width of the patient's own thumb at IP joint). Use reference charts and palpate for the characteristic 'de qi' sensation (local tenderness, aching, or radiating sensation).

Step 2 — Thumb technique: Apply pressure using the pad of the thumb (not the nail); the joint should be slightly flexed, not hyperextended. Body weight should drive the pressure, not wrist force alone.

Step 3 — Pressure parameters: Apply gradual increasing pressure to 3–5 kg force (calibrate on a scale initially to develop proprioceptive reference). Sustain at maximum tolerable depth for 3–5 seconds; release slowly.

Step 4 — Stimulation method: Tonification — slow, sustained, clockwise circular pressure for 30–60 seconds (for deficient conditions: fatigue, weakness). Sedation — faster, anti-clockwise, lighter pressure for 30–60 seconds (for excess conditions: pain, tension, nausea).

Step 5 — Point sequence: Treat bilateral points symmetrically unless unilateral condition warrants ipsilateral or contralateral application. Standard session covers 6–8 points; do not overstimulate (avoid > 12 points per session).

Step 6 — Post-treatment care: Advise patient to rest for 10 minutes; drink 1–2 glasses of water; avoid heavy meals within 1 hour. Document point selection, technique, patient response, and de qi description.
skills.frequency: Daily in acute phase; 3 times per week for chronic maintenance
skills.bnys_tip: The de qi sensation — a dull ache, heaviness, or radiating warmth at the acupressure point — is the clinical confirmation of correct point location and adequate depth of pressure. Teaching patients to identify this sensation empowers them for self-acupressure at home, significantly extending the clinical benefit between sessions.
skills.india_note: Acupressure is widely accepted across India and has strong integration with Siddha medicine point systems in Tamil Nadu and Kerala. Bridging the conceptual link between Chinese meridian points and Ayurvedic marma points for patients enhances acceptance and understanding. Many BNYS curricula in South India explicitly teach this cross-system mapping.

skills.title: Common Emergency Acupressure Points (PC6, ST36, LI4, GV26)
indication: PC6 (Nei Guan): Nausea, vomiting, motion sickness, palpitations, anxiety
ST36 (Zu San Li): Digestive weakness, fatigue, immune support, post-operative nausea
LI4 (He Gu): Headache, facial pain, fever, constipation, labour pain
GV26 (Ren Zhong): Acute loss of consciousness, vasovagal syncope, fainting, sunstroke
contraindication: LI4 — absolute contraindication in pregnancy (strongly emmenagogic)
GV26 — do not apply excessive pressure (risk of nasal septum injury)
PC6 — avoid in patients with active wrist injuries
ST36 — avoid deep pressure in patients with local knee pathology
technique: Step 1 — PC6 (Nei Guan) — Location and technique: Located 3 finger-widths above the wrist crease, between flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus tendons. Apply firm sustained thumb pressure perpendicular to the skin surface; elicit de qi (dull ache radiating to forearm). Apply continuously for 3–5 minutes for acute nausea.

Step 2 — ST36 (Zu San Li) — Location and technique: Located 4 finger-widths below the lower border of the patella, one finger-width lateral to the tibial crest. Apply deep sustained thumb pressure for 2–3 minutes; tonifying effect on digestive qi and immune function. A key point for general vitality in post-illness recovery.

Step 3 — LI4 (He Gu) — Location and technique: Located in the dorsum of the hand, at the midpoint of the 2nd metacarpal bone, on its radial border. Alternatively, at the apex of the fleshy mound when thumb and index finger are approximated. Apply deep pressure with opposite thumb, rotating clockwise; 2–3 minutes for headache relief.

Step 4 — GV26 (Ren Zhong) — Location and technique: Located at the junction of the upper one-third and lower two-thirds of the philtrum (midline between nose and upper lip). In syncope, apply sharp digital pressure (thumbnail or knuckle) with upward angulation for 5–10 seconds until patient responds. This is a BNYS emergency resuscitation point.

Step 5 — Combined emergency protocol: For vasovagal syncope — apply GV26 for immediate response, follow with ST36 bilaterally for 5 minutes to restore circulatory tone. For acute nausea — bilateral PC6 for 5 minutes, followed by ST36 for digestive support.

Step 6 — Safety and documentation: After any emergency point application, monitor vitals; document incident, time, point used, technique, and patient response. Refer to emergency services if response to GV26 is inadequate within 60 seconds.
skills.duration: 3–10 minutes per point per session (emergency use: as needed)
skills.frequency: As clinically required; emergency points — immediate application as needed
skills.bnys_tip: GV26 (Ren Zhong) is the single most important emergency acupressure point that every BNYS clinician must master — it can rapidly reverse vasovagal syncope without medication. Practice point location on yourself and colleagues until it becomes instinctive, as it must be located instantly in an emergency without reference to charts.
skills.india_note: In Indian rural naturopathic clinics with limited emergency drug access, proficiency with GV26 and ST36 is particularly life-saving for managing vasovagal syncope during hydrotherapy procedures. These points should be formally taught and tested as a mandatory clinical competency in BNYS internship programmes.

skills.title: Pain Relief Acupressure Point Protocol
indication: Chronic low back pain — BL23, BL40, GV4
Headache and cervicogenic pain — GB20, LI4, BL10
Knee pain and osteoarthritis — ST34, ST35, SP9, BL40
Menstrual pain and dysmenorrhoea — SP6, CV4, SP10
Sciatica and radicular leg pain — GB30, GB34, BL57
contraindication: SP6 — contraindicated in pregnancy (uterine stimulating)
CV4 — avoid in patients with abdominal aortic aneurysm
GB21 — avoid in pregnancy and post-radical neck dissection
BL40 — avoid in acute popliteal fossa infection or DVT
technique: Step 1 — Clinical pain mapping: Before beginning, complete a pain assessment (VAS 0–10, pain quality, radiation pattern, aggravating/relieving factors). Map the meridian most associated with the pain location to guide point selection.

Step 2 — Distal point first: Always begin with a distal point (below the elbow or knee) before local points — this pre-stimulates the meridian and often immediately reduces local pain intensity. E.g., apply LI4 for cervicogenic headache before local GB20.

Step 3 — Local point technique: Once the meridian has been activated distally, apply pressure to local points; pressure tolerance will typically be higher, and de qi response more prominent. Use sustained circular pressure for 3–5 minutes per point.

Step 4 — Ashi points (tender points): In addition to classical points, identify the point of maximum tenderness (Ashi point) in the pain region and apply gentle sustained pressure for 3–5 minutes. Ashi points are considered direct qi stagnation sites in Chinese medicine.

Step 5 — Bilateral point strategy: For midline pain (low back, headache), stimulate bilateral points simultaneously or alternately. For unilateral pain, apply ipsilateral and contralateral points per clinical protocol.

Step 6 — Post-session VAS reassessment: Re-score pain using VAS immediately post-session and at 30 minutes; document outcome. A minimum 30% VAS reduction is the clinical benchmark for therapeutic response.
skills.frequency: Daily in acute pain phase; 3 times per week for chronic pain
equipment: Acupressure meridian and point reference chart
skills.bnys_tip: The most evidence-supported acupressure pain points are LI4, ST36, and SP6 — multiple RCTs confirm their efficacy for pain, nausea, and dysmenorrhoea respectively. When documenting clinical outcomes in a BNYS setting, using pre/post VAS scores with photographic point location gives your case notes the level of rigour required for clinical audit.
skills.india_note: SP6 (Sanyinjiao) — located 3 cun above the medial malleolus — is one of the most widely used points in BNYS clinical practice in India, particularly for women's health (dysmenorrhoea, PCOS, menstrual irregularity). Its significance is reinforced by parallel use of the equivalent marma point (Gulpha marma region) in Kerala Ayurvedic tradition.

skills.title: Initial Case Taking — Naturopathic History
indication: Complex multi-system presentations requiring holistic evaluation
Preventive health assessment in apparently healthy individuals
contraindication: Acute emergency requiring immediate medical intervention (defer history taking)
Severely cognitively impaired patients (use proxy informant)
Active psychiatric crisis requiring stabilisation first
Language barrier without interpreter (defer until interpreter available)
technique: Step 1 — Chief complaint and history of present illness: Document in patient's own words; explore onset, duration, character, severity (VAS scale), radiation, associated symptoms, aggravating and relieving factors, and previous treatments tried.

Step 2 — Naturopathic lifestyle history: Dietary habits (meal frequency, food types, cooking methods, water intake, junk food, dining out frequency); sleep quality (hours, timing, quality, dreams, nocturnal symptoms); physical activity (type, duration, frequency); occupational history (sedentary vs active, stress levels, screen time).

Step 3 — Elimination pattern history: Bowel frequency and consistency (Bristol Stool Form Scale); urinary frequency and character; sweating pattern; menstrual regularity and symptomatology (for female patients) — these are specific BNYS vital assessment parameters.

Step 4 — Vital force assessment: Naturopathic-specific — assess energy levels throughout the day (morning energy, post-meal energy, evening energy on a 0–10 scale); emotional resilience; social connectedness; purpose and meaning perception.

Step 5 — Past medical, surgical, family, and personal history: Standard medical history including prior investigations and current medications; naturopathic/ayurvedic/homeopathic treatments; family history of lifestyle diseases; social history (family support, living conditions, economic constraints).

Step 6 — Physical examination summary: Complete general and systemic examination documentation; nutritional assessment (BMI, waist circumference, waist-hip ratio); tongue and nail examination (naturopathic diagnostic significance); pulse characteristics.
skills.frequency: Once at initial presentation; abbreviated update at each follow-up
equipment: Bristol Stool Form Scale reference card
skills.bnys_tip: The elimination pattern assessment — bowel frequency, urine character, and sweating — is unique to the BNYS history and reflects the naturopathic emphasis on emunctory function (the body's elimination pathways). A patient reporting constipation, dark urine, and hypohidrosis simultaneously has a significantly compromised elimination capacity that must be addressed before any other treatment is initiated.
skills.india_note: In India, patients frequently underreport dietary details, exercise habits, and sleep quality due to perceived social desirability. Normalising responses by using examples ('many people your age have similar habits...') before asking lifestyle questions significantly improves the accuracy and completeness of the naturopathic history.

skills.title: OARS Motivational Interviewing Technique
indication: Lifestyle modification for chronic disease management
Dietary change and weight management counselling
Physical activity and yoga programme adherence
Emotional eating and disordered eating behaviour
contraindication: Active psychotic episode (defer until stabilised)
Severe cognitive impairment affecting insight
Crisis intervention situation (direct supportive counselling more appropriate)
Patients seeking directive advice only (adjust approach to preference)
technique: Step 1 — O: Open-ended questions: Use questions that cannot be answered with yes or no. Examples: 'What changes would you most like to make to your diet?' / 'How has your health been affecting your daily life?' / 'What have you already tried?' These questions explore the patient's internal motivation without imposing the clinician's agenda.

Step 2 — A: Affirmations: Recognise and affirm the patient's strengths, efforts, and positive intentions. Examples: 'You've shown real commitment by coming in today despite your busy schedule.' Affirmations build therapeutic alliance and counter the shame frequently experienced by patients with lifestyle-related conditions.

Step 3 — R: Reflective listening: Mirror the patient's statements back in a way that confirms understanding and deepens exploration. Simple reflection: 'You feel frustrated that your efforts haven't produced results.' Complex reflection: 'It sounds like you know what to do, but finding the motivation to start is the real challenge.' Aim for 2:1 reflections to questions.

Step 4 — S: Summaries: Periodically summarise the key points discussed, linking positive change-talk with identified barriers. 'So if I understand correctly, you want to improve your diet, you've tried before and found mornings particularly difficult, and having your family's support makes a real difference to your success.'

Step 5 — Explore ambivalence: Use decisional balance technique — ask patient to explore pros and cons of change AND of staying the same. 'What concerns you most about making this change?' followed by 'What motivates you most about making it?'

Step 6 — Elicit commitment: End with an open-ended question to draw out a specific, time-bound commitment from the patient: 'What is one small step you feel confident you could take this week?' Document the patient's self-identified action step in the case notes.
skills.frequency: Initial session + 2 follow-up sessions at 2 and 4 weeks; then monthly
equipment: Comfortable seating (at equal eye level — no doctor above patient hierarchy)
skills.bnys_tip: The most common error in motivational interviewing by BNYS clinicians is the 'righting reflex' — the instinct to immediately correct, advise, or educate when a patient resists change. MI requires the practitioner to resist this reflex and roll with resistance instead. Resistance is not a patient failure; it is clinical information about ambivalence that needs to be explored with curiosity, not overcome with authority.
skills.india_note: In the Indian clinical context, patients from rural areas and lower socioeconomic strata may expect directive counselling from a doctor and may initially perceive MI's collaborative approach as the clinician being unknowledgeable or unhelpful. Begin these sessions with a brief explanation of why you are asking for their perspective, normalising the collaborative model.

skills.title: Treatment Compliance Counselling
indication: Patients with established pattern of non-adherence
Long-term dietary and lifestyle modification programmes
Home hydrotherapy or yoga prescription adherence
Chronic disease management requiring continuous lifestyle change
contraindication: Acute illness phase (defer compliance counselling until stable)
Literacy barriers (adapt to verbal/visual counselling tools)
Active depression severely impairing executive function
Cultural or family barriers requiring systemic intervention first
technique: Step 1 — Non-judgmental assessment of compliance: Ask 'On the past 7 days, how many days were you able to follow the prescribed treatment plan?' — use a scale (0–7 days) to quantify compliance without judgment. Normalise partial compliance: 'Most people find it challenging to follow everything perfectly.'

Step 2 — Barrier identification: Explore specific barriers — physical (time, energy, pain), logistical (equipment availability, cost, travel to clinic), social (family understanding, work schedule), and psychological (motivation, belief in treatment efficacy).

Step 3 — Problem-solving for barriers: Collaboratively generate 2–3 practical solutions for each identified barrier. Document agreed solutions in writing — a written action plan significantly improves follow-through compared to verbal-only instruction.

Step 4 — Treatment simplification: Review the current prescription for complexity. Apply the KISS principle (Keep It Simple and Specific) — prescribe the minimum effective intervention that the patient can realistically sustain. Incremental compliance with 2 practices is superior to full non-compliance with 8.

Step 5 — Self-monitoring tools: Provide a simple daily treatment diary or habit tracker; weekly self-check-in via clinic WhatsApp group (with patient consent) for low-cost accountability. Review the diary at each visit without criticism.

Step 6 — Positive reinforcement: At each follow-up, begin by noting and affirming what the patient did achieve, regardless of how small. Reinforce internal motivation ('You mentioned that when you do the yoga in the morning, you feel more energetic — that's your body telling you it works') rather than external obligation.
skills.frequency: At every follow-up visit, until compliance is consistently established
equipment: Visual instruction cards for home practices
WhatsApp group or SMS reminder system (optional)
skills.bnys_tip: Research on behaviour change shows that patient compliance with a prescription is directly proportional to the degree to which the patient was involved in creating it. Co-prescribe rather than prescribe — ask the patient 'Which of these two options do you think you could manage?' rather than dictating the treatment plan. Ownership drives adherence.
skills.india_note: WhatsApp has effectively replaced telephone follow-up in Indian clinical practice. A well-managed patient WhatsApp group for BNYS compliance support — with daily reminders for yoga, diet, and hydration — is a low-cost, high-impact intervention that is particularly effective with the Indian patient population's high smartphone adoption rate.

skills.title: SOAP Note Writing for BNYS
indication: Daily clinical documentation for all inpatient BNYS consultations
Clinical audit and research data collection
contraindication: Not applicable — documentation is mandatory for all clinical encounters
Note: Emergency situations require abbreviated documentation first, complete SOAP note retrospectively within 24 hours
technique: Step 1 — S (Subjective): Record the patient's own report of their symptoms and experience since last visit. Include: current chief complaints (verbatim where possible), changes in symptoms, response to previous treatments, sleep quality, energy levels, bowel/urinary function, dietary adherence, exercise compliance, and emotional well-being. Always include quotes to preserve patient voice.

Step 2 — O (Objective): Record measurable clinical findings — vitals (BP, PR, RR, temperature, weight, SpO₂), physical examination findings relevant to the case, clinical test results, and naturopathic assessment parameters (tongue characteristics: colour, coating, moisture; nail signs; visible skin changes; abdominal palpation findings).

Step 3 — A (Assessment): Clinical interpretation of subjective and objective data. Document: problem list (active diagnoses with BNYS perspective), response to current treatment (improving/unchanged/worsening), any new clinical concerns identified, and revised differential diagnoses if relevant.

Step 4 — P (Plan): Document today's treatment administered (list all hydrotherapy, mud therapy, massage, yoga, dietary interventions with precise parameters). Prescriptions issued (diet changes, home practices). Investigations ordered. Referrals made. Follow-up schedule. Patient education provided.

Step 5 — BNYS-specific documentation additions: Include naturopathic vital force assessment (improvement in energy, emotional wellbeing, elimination), nutrition diary review findings, yoga practice compliance, and any healing crisis documentation.

Step 6 — Completion standards: Sign and date all entries with name, qualification, and designation. No abbreviations without prior definition. Corrections must be single-line strikethrough with initials — never white-out. In electronic systems, avoid copy-paste of previous notes without active review.
skills.frequency: Every clinical encounter without exception
equipment: Standardised BNYS SOAP note form (printed or EMR template)
skills.bnys_tip: The most common SOAP note failure in BNYS practice is a Plan section that lists only treatments administered today without forward planning or clinical reasoning. The Plan should answer: what did we do, why, and what is the intended clinical outcome with a measurable benchmark? This transforms documentation from a billing record into a genuine clinical thinking tool.
skills.india_note: Many BNYS colleges and naturopathic hospitals in India are transitioning to digital health records. Familiarity with basic EMR systems (including HIS platforms used in Ayush hospitals) is increasingly required for BNYS graduates seeking hospital employment. Maintain the habit of complete SOAP documentation regardless of platform.

skills.title: Clinical Photography Standards for BNYS
indication: Skin disorders — psoriasis, eczema, acne, fungal infections (before/after documentation)
Nutritional deficiency signs — nail, hair, skin documentation
Progress documentation for research and audit
contraindication: Without explicit written informed consent — clinical photography is never performed
Minors — require parental/guardian written consent
Images of intimate areas require specific dignity protocols and same-gender photographer
Never photograph for non-clinical purposes or share without specific anonymisation and consent
technique: Step 1 — Informed consent: Obtain written informed consent specifically for clinical photography, explaining purpose (clinical record, research, teaching), storage, access, and that consent can be withdrawn at any time. Use a specific photography consent form separate from general treatment consent.

Step 2 — Standardisation protocol: For serial documentation (pre/post), maintain identical conditions — same camera, same distance, same angle, same lighting (natural diffuse light or identical artificial setup), same patient positioning, same time of day. Standardisation is what makes serial photography clinically meaningful.

Step 3 — Identification without faces: Where possible, exclude the face from clinical photographs (e.g., postural photography, skin lesion photography). Where the face must be included (tongue diagnosis, facial skin conditions), obtain specific face photography consent and apply anonymisation before any sharing.
Step 4 — Reference scale inclusion: Include a ruler or coin of known size in photographs of skin lesions and localised conditions to permit objective size documentation and comparison.

Step 5 — Tongue photography standard: Patient seated in neutral position, natural or consistent artificial lighting (no flash directly in mouth), tongue protruded to 75% of maximum extent without straining. Photograph within 30 seconds of tongue protrusion (colour and coating change with prolonged protrusion).

Step 6 — Storage and security: Store clinical images in encrypted, secure clinic storage (not personal phone gallery). Label with patient ID (not name), date, and anatomical site. Retain as part of medical record for the mandatory clinical documentation retention period (as per Indian Medical Council regulations — minimum 3 years, recommended 7 years).
skills.frequency: Baseline at admission; at 2-week intervals; at discharge; at 3-month follow-up
equipment: Dedicated clinic smartphone or camera (not personal device)
Consistent lighting setup (ring light or diffuser)
Photo documentation form (patient ID, date, site)
skills.bnys_tip: For tongue diagnosis documentation, establish a dedicated tongue photography protocol at your clinic — consistent chair position, headrest, and a ring light with neutral colour temperature (5000K). Tongue colour interpretation is highly influenced by lighting; without standardised conditions, serial tongue photographs are diagnostically unreliable.
skills.india_note: India's Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act (DISHA) framework, when enacted, will impose strict regulations on clinical image storage and sharing. Currently, follow ICMR Guidelines for Biomedical Research (2017) and ensure clinical photographs are stored on clinic servers, not on WhatsApp or Google Photos, which are non-compliant with clinical data security standards.

skills.title: Progress Note Template for BNYS Inpatient
indication: Daily documentation for all BNYS inpatients
Monitoring treatment response to multi-modal therapy
Communication between morning and evening duty doctors
contraindication: Not applicable — progress notes are mandatory documentation for all inpatient encounters
Note: If a patient's condition changes acutely, a separate urgent clinical note must be written in addition to the regular progress note
technique: Step 1 — Day header: Date, time, day number of admission (e.g., Day 7 of 14), and the treating doctor's name and designation. This contextualises the note within the treatment timeline.

Step 2 — Symptom response section: Using a standardised set of BNYS outcome parameters: pain VAS score (0–10), energy level score (0–10), sleep quality score (1–5), bowel frequency and Bristol type (1–7), appetite (good/fair/poor), emotional well-being (stable/improved/distressed). These parameters provide a rapid longitudinal clinical trend without lengthy narrative.

Step 3 — Vital signs and weight: BP (supine and seated if orthostasis relevant), PR, RR, temperature, SpO₂ (if respiratory condition), body weight (daily for detox and weight management programmes). Note significant trends with directional indicators (↑↓ →).

Step 4 — Naturopathic clinical assessment: Brief tongue observation (colour, coating change); significant palpation findings (abdominal, joint); skin changes (improvement in dermatological conditions); urine and stool observation (colour, consistency).

Step 5 — Today's treatment administered: Concise list with parameters — 'Hip bath (cold, 15°C, 10 min); Abdominal mud pack (20 min); Foot reflexology (35 min); Yoga class (45 min); Juice: ash gourd 200 mL × 3.' This provides a clear record of daily therapeutic load.

Step 6 — Assessment and plan modification: Brief clinical impression and any modifications to the treatment plan — 'Patient improving; advancing from juice to fruit diet from tomorrow. Reducing hip bath frequency to alternate days due to adequate bowel response achieved.' Sign, date, and time the entry.
skills.frequency: Once daily (minimum); twice daily for complex or acute cases
equipment: Structured BNYS progress note form with pre-printed scoring fields
Bristol Stool Form Scale reference card
skills.bnys_tip: The most efficient BNYS progress note uses a structured hybrid format — pre-printed scoring fields for daily outcome parameters (eliminates repetitive writing) combined with a brief free-text assessment and plan. Design your clinic's progress note template with these fields pre-printed; it reduces documentation time by 50% while improving data quality for audit.
skills.india_note: In Indian BNYS inpatient facilities, the handover between morning and evening duty doctors is a critical clinical safety point. A well-structured progress note that clearly identifies the most important clinical development of the day (e.g., 'healing crisis today — patient weak, hypotensive, monitor closely tonight') ensures continuity of care, particularly in facilities with high patient-to-doctor ratios.
